text: Here we present a case of a forty-eight-year-old male with invasive rectal adenocarcinoma, stage T-N-N-zero, who received neoadjuvant chemoradiation and subsequently underwent low anterior resection and transanal total mesorectal excision of the rectum with hand-sewn colloanal anastomosis and diverting loop ileostomy. Four months after his index procedure, the patient presented with prolapse of his neorectum of approximately three to four centimeters, leading to drainage and discomfort. The patient was taken to the operating room for planned reduction of his prolapse with neorectopexy. The patient was placed on the table in supine position, prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion, and the robot docked.

The surgeon performs extensive circumferential dissection to free pelvic adhesions around the neorectum. The neorectum is fully mobilized and reduced into the abdominal cavity without tension. A tuovicral stitch is placed in the serosa of the neorectum and sutured to the anterior sacral ligament.

The surgeon performs additional dissection to ensure tension-free reduction of the neorectum. A 2-0 V-Lock stitch is placed in the serosa and sutured to the anterior sacral ligament for added support. The suture is tied, completing the neorectopexy. The repair is inspected, and hemostasis is achieved.
At three-month follow-up, the patient was symptom-free, having normal bowel function, and underwent elective reversal of his prior ileostomy.
